description: Thursday 19 June 2025 | Foreign Affairs, Defence and Trade Committee 2025/26 Estimates for Vote Customs Hon Casey Costello, Minister of Customs New Zealand Customs Service Jamie Bamford, Acting Comptroller of the New Zealand Customs Service and Deputy Chief Executive Operations Richard Bargh, Deputy Chief Executive Policy, Legal and Strategy 2025/26 Estimates for Vote Defence and Vote Defence Force Hon Judith Collins KC, Minister of Defence Hon Chris Penk, Associate Minister of Defence and Minster for Veterans Ministry of Defence Brook Barrington, Secretary of Defence Sarah Minson, Deputy Secretary Capability Delivery Pasanka Wickremasinghe, Chief Financial Officer New Zealand Defence Force Air Marshall Tony Davies, Chief of Defence Force Alex Brunt, Acting Head of Veterans’ Affairs New Zealand 2025/26 Estimates for Vote Defence Force Hon Chris Penk, Minister for Veterans New Zealand Defence Force Air Marshall Tony Davies, Chief of Defence Force Alex Brunt, Acting Head of Veterans’ Affairs New Zealand
descriptionSummary: 2025/26 Estimates for Vote Customs. Hon Casey Costello, Minister of Customs. New Zealand Customs Service

chunks.text: Just let everyone file in and then we'll wrap into it.

Alright, well, good afternoon, minister. Welcome along to the Foreign Affairs Defense and Trade Committee for this afternoon's hearing on Vote Customs. Uh, we have an hour set down for today's hearing. We will hand over to you for some introductory comments, and then I'm sure there'll be a range of questions. Thank you very much. I'd first like to acknowledge the customs officials here today to help me in this role, but thank you, Mr.

Chair. Um, I think just to set the scene, um, customs, as you know, um, is responsible for the collection of revenue supporting trade, protecting our border, and facilitating travel. So they are a very busy unit. Um, I'll briefly comment on a couple of areas I'm sure the committee will want to ask more about. Um, first of all, customs is stopping an unprecedented quantity of illegal drugs nearly five tons in the past 12 months.

Over the past 10 years, seizures of methamphetamine have increased nearly tenfold to around 2,500 kilograms in 2024 calendar year. The pattern is just as stark when looking at interceptions of meth destined for New Zealand offshore seizures have gone from 10 kilograms in 2016 to around 2,300 kilograms last year.

Cocaine seizures are smaller in scale compared to methamphetamine, but have significantly increased in recent years. Just last month, customs seized almost 300 kilograms of cocaine of two ships that arrived at the port of toga via Panama. These patterns are part of the context for the government supporting customs with an extra 35 million in this year's budget, this will target more offenders involved in smaller to medium quantity, high frequency drug smuggling, who we know aspire to be the big players in the future.

The funding also aims to boost the ability to identify and prosecute those trusted insiders who use their access and ports or airports to facilitate criminal activity and will allow customs to put additional effort into helping raise awareness with the private sector partners and build a broader response to organized crime.

The extra money will also fund an additional overseas customs liaison officer adding to the agency's network of international intelligence sharing capabilities that help stop drugs reaching New Zealand. A lot of work is going on around improving the broader government response to transnational serious and organized crime and customs is providing the secretariat support to the trans transnational and serious organized Crime Ministerial Advisory Group, which I'm happy to discuss later.

While border protection is understandably a major focus, there are other significant pieces of work underway. One of the most important of these is the review of customs fees to recover the cost of managing and processing goods across our border. Changes being made to these fees and the structure of the fees regime will ensure costs are recovered more fairly sustainably, and remove taxpayer subsidization.

New rates for goods management fees will take effect from one July with structural changes happening from one April next year. Another piece of work in the area of customs trade, which is happening towards the end of this year, is a new time release survey that measures customs clearance times for goods. The last survey was carried out in 2023 and showed customs cleared nearly 94% of sea cargo containers before arrival in New Zealand.

Over 78% of air cargo imports entries were cleared before arrival. Reflecting the much shorter time, time travels or air freight. A hundred percent of air cargo exports were cleared before leaving New Zealand. One more example of the high quality service customs delivers and its commitment to protecting, um, commitment to protect and promote New Zealand's interests across the borders, whether to take any questions.

Excellent. Thank you Council. Jenny Solicitor minister, your ministerial advisory group on transnational, um, serious and organized crime. We've, uh, seen three reports March, April, and may. They describe a customs border system that is overwhelmed by record drug imports. You and your, um, your opening statements actually talked about the huge, uh, drugs that customs have seized, but we also see corruption, according to your ministerial, uh, advisory group's report, insider threats failed interagency coordination.

Do you accept that under your leadership New Zealand is losing the fight against transnational organized crime? And if not, why? I I think I'd put some, um, context around the commentary from the reports. Sure. Excuse me. The Ministerial Advisory Group was tasked to be bold to be, um, an analytical in its approach.

Um, we have, um, and I think if you put the reports in context around the challenges that we are facing, it is not talking about customs failing. It's talking about New Zealand's response overall in the world's climate. So I think that's, um, important to make that context. The part that the, uh, the group is focused on is how do the agencies connect together better?

How do they work more cooperatively and what more can we do? I think the report fairly reflects the incredible work policing customs are doing. How we are seizing more than we've ever seized before. What we are focused on is who the enemy is, which is the international organized criminal cartels that are targeting New Zealand, and therefore the focus of the group is what do we as a country do to be better at it?

And so I would strongly defend the fact that we are not delivering. I think we can name numerous operations that the customs and police and other agencies have done. What we know is we can do more and we can do better. And from my position, I believe New Zealand should be the very best in the world at this because we have the advantage of a single border.

We have a geographic isolation, we have a single enforcement agencies, we have a single jurisdiction. So therefore, how we connect up and what we do, and that's what the ministerial Advisory Group has been set up to do, is to challenge what we're doing now, analyze what we're doing now, and identify what we can do better. Supplementary Mr. Chair, um, let me reframe the question.

I wasn't actually pointing the finger at customs. The organization failing, um, may merely pointing out that ministerial advisory group is just that it provides advice, but the person with ministerial mandate to act minister is yourself. So when we look at your March report, um, one of the graphs actually that the March report has basically gives us the picture that meth amphetamine use as per the wastewater, has increased over the last year by nearly a hundred percent.

So why is it that even though there are all these drugs being seized, we see from the wastewater, um, usage of, of people in New Zealand, it's actually doubled. Doesn't that mean that actually the border is not as safe and secure as it should be Minister, which is one of your core roles?

No, I would say that the border is, we can always improve, we can do better. The consumption component that you're talking about is there's, as you know, customs protects the border, the consumption component is at a police level in terms of, you know, the enforcement of, um, the usage and possession and in the community level.

And there's a, um, um, a number of initiatives that happen, including the resilience, organized crime and communities. Since we started to become aware of the wastewater results. Um, customs responded very quickly. Um, the budget 2025 bid, as you can appreciate, commenced last year.

Um, as you know, the timing of those budget bids, we also, um, commenced work on the border security bill, which is coming through and being developed at the moment. So there was a significant amount of work in how we can do things within customs to strengthen. Um, we worked on a number of relationships with the ports. We, um, launched, um, the purchase program with Navy to purchase the, um, UNC crewed surface vessel and started exploring UNC crewed air vessels with the, um, view of strengthening our water response.

Um, I think that from the moment that we became aware there was some issues, and that's on top of the already very vigilant work. And as I've said, you know, 10 years ago we were seizing, um, 55 kilograms of methamphetamine in a year. Last year we seized 90 kilograms of meth a week.

So I think that we have an exemplary force, but it does tell us that there is more we can do. And we're having a very frank and honest conversation with all of the agencies about what more we can do to reduce that impact. There is always more that you can do minister, but one of the things that we know, um, in terms of the staff who are at the border in terms of the effect of some of those cuts, we're actually seeing the staff cuts that have been confirmed is about 79 people right at customs.

Um, but the numbers are actually worse than that because the numbers I've got back through from written questions is that there's been 120 positions disestablished at customs. And if you add on the unfilled positions, which is 111 positions, and you then look at this graph of methamphetamine and went when it went up in terms of construction, you could basically see when there is a correlation between this meth use and over 200 staff of customs not actually being their minister.

So yes, you have a ministerial advisory group that is there now actually to actually advise you, but minister, you have the role as the minister to act instead of just waiting for the ministerial advisory group to give you recommendations. And, and as I've said, um, you minister chair that I can acknowledge that we have acted.

Um, I can go through all of the, um, operations that we are currently doing, the initiatives that we have driven from a customs perspective. Um, further to that is that as we responded in all of the written questions, no frontline positions that are involved in the direct enforcement and control of our border.

We are disestablished. In fact, we've had significant investment in technology. We've had significant investment in the capacity that customs has to respond, including our international relationships, including the, the intelligence gathering and analytics. As you've seen, we've opened the new, um, the new NZ post open, the new, um, mail center and, um, mail for data.

Uh, data for mail has been a project of great, um, success in terms of being able to analyze a lot more as what's coming in the border. So because of those technology advancements, because of the way that we are using information, we have been able to be a good agency in terms of reducing surplus staff and redirecting that energy into the frontline staff, as you've suggested we needed to do to strengthen that order.

So those positions, although just established, did not alter our capacity to control the border. And in fact, our visibility to control the border has been strengthened. Okay. We have supplementary questions from Tim Costley, Hernandez and Laura McClure start. Tim. Thanks Mr. Chair. Thank you, Vanessa. I'm quite extraordinary. Really. I trying to, I, I want come back to the first question, though.

It seems like the member has backpedaled dramatically from it, from what I took as a, a massive criticism of custom stuff, actually, I think, and I just want have recorded now that your, your staff are on the board are doing an amazing job. We've seen some record, um, and things over the last couple of years. And I, I just, for, for those that actually put on the uniform, we can airports, our ports that are out at sea combating this stuff.

I just wanna, uh, record now a a vote of thanks at least from one side of the table here. 'cause we appreciate what the work that they do. Um, and we have seen some, some great results as I referred to. But, but, but I wanna reflect on the original question, which was the, the ministerial advisory, um, panel that you had set up and these issues, which, I mean, in one sense it's great that they've been identified now because now you can have a work plan, but did they just pop up overnight or were they actually the issues that were there prior

to the last election as well just ticking along blind? Yeah, I think that's, that's a, um, a part of, um, the reason why the work is so important. I think it, um, as we worked through and, and full acknowledgement to the expertise within the advisory group, they all are very experienced in their own, um, fields of expertise.

But they, um, it is this, the way in which government has really responded in an enforcement process. Customs and Police over a number of years have been probably the leaders in that cooperative relationship. Customs has been, um, you know, really grateful for the role with defense, but as we've seen, there's a lot more we can do to strengthen that relationship.

So I think over time we have, um, and that's part of what the advisory group is saying. We, we have tended to operate very siloed. Um, so IRD does their part, immigration does their part, police does their part, custom does their part. What we want to see now is how that cohesion works. And in our response to organized crime, we've tended to move at the pace of law.

Um, the criminal element moves at the pace of money. So they are very nimble, very responsive, and wherever there's a weak link, they will, um, expose it. So this is this connection over a long period of time. Governments have operated like that, I think is, Is that unique to New Zealand? What we are seeing in terms of drugs being moved?

Are we, we the only country in the world experiencing that? No. And if you look at those results around the world, they're all seeing those massive spikes. Australia is in the same position. Um, I'm aware the advisory group was just in a Australia recently. Um, the same sort of measures. New Zealand is quite unique in terms of the way we do the wastewater testing. Um, so we have a very, um, very robust system.

So we have very real time data. So, um, I think the last report has shown a, a little bit of a leveling off, which is encouraging, but still nowhere near where we want it to be. So I think we have, um, the benefit of really good data, um, but we are not unique around the world. Francisco Hernandez, uh, thank you, Mr. Chair.

I'm interested in following on from line of inquiry that, um, the member, the Honorable Jenny Sesa, um, articulated and also noting, um, to the member Tim Ey, that, you know, we all know and acknowledge the good work that customs do, uh, the differences that members on this side haven't implemented.

I'm not the one in the committee here implemented that question, and I suggest you target it to the ministry, two customs, unlike the government on that bench over here. So one of the, tell them now, tell them what a great job they're doing right now. Well, yeah, you guys are doing a fantastic job. Unfortunately, the government has cut some, has made baseline saving targets, cuts, which has clearly degraded the capacity of customs, despite assertions to the contrary.

And one of those things has been the, um, spending of nearly around $5 million in redundancy since 27th of November, uh, 2023, as well as four redundancy processes. Can you talk through what the impact that has had in terms of, uh, staff morale, in terms of disruption, in terms of, um, time being spent in dealing with redundancies rather than doing the real mahi that customs needs to do?

And so far as it relates To the Estimates looking forward? Yeah, so I, I would suggest that, um, and every agency that that runs through this process, I think it's, um, credit to the organization that they are constantly looking at opportunities to improve overall customs, um, has a very low staff retention rate and, uh, very, very, um, low staff turnover rate.

They have, um, in terms of government agencies, they have good retention of staff. So I think that reflects well in, you know, your measure of morale. Um, I think that your statement around, um, that we have undermined or compromised, despite my assertions that is, you know, untrue, we have not undermined our frontline capacity to respond.

Those assertions are not false. They're absolutely true. We have not undermined our frontline capability to deliver custom services. There has been staff restructures. There will always be staff restructures across agencies in order to ensure you're efficient. As I've said, some of those savings result on the fact that we have been able to produce a lot of, um, efficiencies through the use of technology.

Um, the traveler declaration, which was implemented has also created some savings, the ability to use, um, data and analytics, um, of the movement of cargo. I think you have to also appreciate that customs is not just an enforcement agency. They support trade, they support travel. Um, there is a lot of roles that are not involved in border enforcement, and I think that's the collective size of the organization and recognition of the work it does.

Okay. Supplementary Laura McClure. Thank you, Mr. Chair. Um, just for the benefit of the committee, um, I think some of the members might not understand supply and demand when it comes to, to drug use in this country. Um, and custom's role is really seizing at the border and trying to prevent it entering, but this is a much larger issue.

And in your capacity, obviously describing the problems, um, and how you see them, how is the work with police going and how do you feel like, um, we have the base improvements and the best way that you could target that, and how do you feel that it's progressing at this point? So I think the cooperative relationship, um, between police and customs, and I think if I could, you know, highlight one recent operation, which was Operation gida, um, which you would've seen in the media, the steel beams that were, um, that was one of the, like those key examples

of that cooperative relationship where customs identified it. Um, they organized the control delivery and then police came in and monitored and watched. So not only did we get the drugs, we got all the offenders in the ring involved in it. So it was a tremendous, rather protracted, but really effective relationships. So at that level, we know we can do more.

Um, this week, um, there was the, uh, transnational organized crime, um, forum of the first two days of this week where all of the agencies come together, but police definitely leads. Um, I think I've got a significant advantage in the fact that I have, um, both the police delegation organized crime as well as customs, um, the cooperative relationship.

And that's where the funding around, um, the work that customs is doing has been so important in this budget because it is a significant investment in intelligence as well as staffing. Um, being able to target that frontline. Uh, the border security bill that I mentioned before is another way where we are looking at opportunities to strengthen legislation to increase the power for customs as we move forward.

Um, and that compliments work that police do, um, in their legislation so that we can work more cohesively together. So moving forward, there is quite a bit of work around that cooperation. Um, and that's, I, I think the example that we're trying to set for that cooperative relationship amongst the other agencies. Thank you. Final supplementary. Thank you.

Um, I just think it's worth noting also that I'm just interested your view, uh, but that there is a significant amount of money in this budget for, uh, in the 20 25, 26 budget for, uh, strengthening the border, uh, $29 million extra going in. And I'm just interested to know, um, what, what initiatives or how that will be, um, spent over the course of the four year appropriation to Okay.

To help fight this particular issue. Thank you. Um, importantly, one of the main, main initiatives is, will be an increase in staff, and that is the increase in staff at a frontline level position. Um, we are looking at probably about 60 positions will be created through that. Um, the other component is to actually use that staff to target that small to medium, um, quantity of goods.

So that is that, um, the, the air freight, the airport, um, we've seen a massive increase in, um, couriers through the airport, um, seized unaccompanied luggage as well as those, um, couriers bringing, um, so having that, um, presence, frontline presence will be really important. Uh, the others is using the intelligence component, but also, um, creating a, an additional overseas role, which will be, um, really critically important from a customer's perspective is our international relationships.

So having those presence and having met with those staff overseas, um, it is hugely beneficial for us to have frontline New Zealand, um, enforcement agencies are highly respected overseas. Um, and we've actually responded to that, um, by, you know, the, the request for having more presence in this budget will deliver that as well.

Awesome. Thank you. Right. Next question. Honorable Damian O'Connor. Okay, Jenny, uh, supplementary actually, in terms of the staff. So in terms of your new, um, money this year for, um, customs, as you've just explained, 60 new positions, but minister, that's only half of the 120 positions that have been disestablished at customs between January 20, 24 and 9th of June, 2025.

That's actually not my question though. My question is with regards to this, um, report from the DEA, um, ministry of Justice, US Department of Justice, which actually gave us the warning, this is way back in November, 2024, that Mexican cartels, uh, present here in, in New Zealand and Australia, not news to you.

Um, because we, this presence has actually been felt, uh, for some time. What we do know, though, this is from your, um, advisory reports, is their presence is now really, really increased, um, these, uh, drug cartels from as far away as South America. So, um, what are you doing in terms of ensuring that corruption ports at customs?

What vetting have you directed, um, the comp controller to do, to ensure that there is better oversight, um, at the border? Because what we know from the reports is that about 70% of insider threats is actually via bribery and corruption. So, and that's how have you directed our controller to ensure that that corruption is actually not coming through and bringing illicit drugs through from these drug cartels.

So I think it's un appreciate the, the process by which, um, you know, the, the ministerial, um, responsibility is to set expectations and I've set those expectations. Um, I think the customs, um, the comp controller and the customs, um, team collectively have been very proactive.

And I, and you will have seen that in the, the responses to the questions you've put around the processes that they have for managing, um, potential corruption weaknesses. The vetting processes have all been clearly outlined for you. I'm happy to rever to Jamie to kind of detail what that actually looks like, but I'm very confident that there is significant, um, um, controls and, um, feelings put in place to make sure that they weakness.

I think I would highlight that when we talk about corruption and a trusted insider, we are not purely talking about government employees, as you'll see in the report, and you're seen in the responses that you've seen the trusted insiders are everyone involved in the supply chain? Mm. So it can be baggage handlers, it can be ticket issuers, it can be, um, freight forwarding companies.

It can be a range of initiatives. Everyone that has a role to play in the supply chain that allows goods and products to be entering this country can be deemed as a trusted insider. And so therefore, the response that we have is not just about what we do within government agencies and this additional presence and this additional staff.

And again, I'll go back to the 60 extra staff or 60 extra staff in the positions that we need them to be. The redundancies that we went through were restructuring positions that weren't where we needed them to be and weren't delivering what we needed from an organization. This additional response is about putting positions where they really need to be and delivering what we really want to strengthen our border.

And part of that presence is how we intersect on a trusted insider program. There is roles that we have to ensure that those things like unaccompanied baggage, uh, being monitored and controlled, and that there is a physical presence where it needs to be to deter that trusted insider.

On top of that, the border security bill will ensure that customs have a bit more power and control over the environments where our ports and ports need to be. Can I just have a supplementary to do, um, given that, you know, you talk about everyone in in supply chain, I guess the trust, um, over the last 18 months we've seen New Zealand's reputation, uh, or ranking in terms of corruption internationally slip, and I guess that the transnational organized crime would be part of that, your influence across that supply chain.

So there was a strategy in place, so why did you choose to park that strategy, not follow through, and what were the issues that you had with it? Because there was a 20 to 25 transnational organized crime strategy wasn't there, and that was being followed and that has been parked as I understand. So Yeah.

The issues with it. Yeah, I appreciate the question. Thank you. We are not, um, we haven't refreshed the strategy. So the strategy as it's in place, so what we're working through now is the refresher, the strategy will occur, um, this year. So we haven't, we're not walked away from that strategy position. Um, what we are working towards is position Or implementation, Sorry, the implementation Of that strategy.

I mean, have you followed through? Yes, We have. Yeah, sorry. Yeah, so the strategy has been, but what we are wanting to do now is refresh the strategy because as, as you can appreciate when it was developed, it was a, a slightly different environment to what we're dealing with now, as we've seen the massive spike in the last year. Um, and the, the way the cartels and the out out, you know, the threat is attacking us.

So this is the opportunity to use the advisory group to look at what everyone else is doing, what our agencies are doing, and so that the strategy becomes more broadly applied across government, um, and how we can do better in our response. It really isn't that that strategy was wrong, it's just that the environment changes so much.

We wanna make sure that that strategy is in line with where we are heading. And I think that's the opportunity we've got. Um, so the timing of the advisory group reporting back in September with their final report and the refresh of the strategy and potentially budget bids moving forward, should all line up through that process. Supplementary from Tim Costley?

Yeah, just coming back to that sort of suggestion that you haven't done anything, I'm, I'm reading the, uh, estimates here on page five of the vote customs. Uh, and I noticed that you by the look of this, I want to ask if this is correct and what the money will be used for. So there's two parts, but from what, what I'm reading it here, it looks like you have secured under new PO policy initiatives, uh, new money for combating transnational and serious organized crime at the border.

Two and a half million in the next financial year, then four and a half, 9 million in 27, 28, and a further 13.2 million. So that's 29.2 million over the next four years. And on top of that, I'm reading five and a half million in capital injection on top of that. Is that, is that correct? Is that you have actually secured even in these, the, the difficult economy extra money for this.

And are those fundings gonna be used as, as I have read it, but you may have more information to be targeting those involved in log qua quantity, high frequency smuggling, such as, uh, you know, mail centers or airports. Uh, also that it will be used to improve supply chain security and targeting the trusted insiders that we've been hearing the questions about who are using those links to smuggle drugs.

And finally, is it, will it involve extending customs presence overseas funding a a, an additional liaison officer to support those investigations offshore and make sure we have the best information? So I wonder if you have comment? Yeah, so, and as you see, because it's a, a phased in funding as, as we bring the staff up to strength, um, that that will be a big component.

I think I can refer to Jamie in terms of, 'cause he, he delivers the operational side of it, but the, the, the big driver is around building that workforce cap capability over the next few years. But the international relationships and some of that work had already started. Um, this is about, you know, there was some temporary reassignment of staff in order to, to respond to, to test that process.

Um, that's been led really effectively at the airport. But then as we move forward now, this is about we can do this permanently. We can plan, um, a bigger presence. Um, that's the people presence as well as those additional roles. I think as, as we've said, we have stopped before, they've reached the country more, almost as many, much drugs as we've stopped at our border.

So those international relationships become so really critically important, um, those international relationships. But I might ask Jamie just to comment on, um, that, how that staffing or how that function will be delivered. Thank you, minister. Yes. So it's, uh, just shy of $35 million over four years. We grow to 60 people over those four years. It's not the full 29 million, 30 million in, in year one.

Yeah. Uh, so we will kind of grow as, uh, over that time. Um, it's more intelligence, it's more investigators as the minister says, uh, offshore presence to drive investigations and disrupt drugs offshore. There's money there for technology, um, software and, um, uh, and also a frontline, uh, uh, airport border enforcement element, um, that where the uniform at our airports to, um, basically disrupt inside a threat, uh, that we're, that we're seeing.

So that comes across over four years. Thank you. A Supplementary from Laura McClure. Yeah. Thank you, Mr. Chair. Um, given, I mean, I'm interested to know your thoughts, minister, and potentially Jamie, because you have been working in this area a while. Do you think, why do you think New Zealand's been targeted by these cartels? And do you think that domestically over the last sort of six or so years, um, local activity had really increased and just the networks between, say our local gangs and the cartels has, has become quite strengthened.

Um, why do you think that New Zealand has been targeted? I, I think I can lead in first and then Jamie. So one of the things we saw, you know, post COVID, there was a real shift, the organized crime or the gang presence in New Zealand, you know, really did, um, blow out.

We know that we have the highest retail price for methamphetamine. So in terms of world market mm-hmm. There's a lot of money to be made here. So that makes us a very attractive market to be in because of the sale price, right? We know, um, from intelligence that there had been a fair amount of stock piling of drugs so that, you know, because they were able, they, they operate like a business.

So they do operate and control release to that because they wanna high retain that high volume of money internationally. This, the, the environment had changed significantly and that connection, um, through Pacific and through, um, in, into South America. And um, you know, there, there had been a lot of, um, um, connection with that sort of presence here in New Zealand, which happened, um, you know, over the, over recent years.

So that kind of relationship and it is a business model and that's what we need to get through. Mm-hmm. The other thing, just before I hand it to Jamie, is that we've gotta recognize that from a organized crime perspective, they don't really care what the commodity is, it's about what they make money on. Mm. So it can be money laundering, it can be, you know, um, um, drugs, it can be people trafficking, it can be scamming.

It's, there's anything from which they can make money, they will profit. And that's why it's across agencies so important. Yeah. But I think Jamie might be in better position to say what changed. I'd say thank you, minister. I'd say kind of broadly for methamphetamine and some other drugs, one, um, kind of the way the world is, it has become easier and cheaper to manufacture methamphetamine.

If you think cocaine, you need to grow it on a hill, you have all those kind of enforcement activities that are spraying or doing whatever. Actually you can create meth in a warehouse, in a cellar, whatever, very, very, very cheaply. Right? Some countries, uh, particularly Mexico, Myanmar actually the rule of law is challenged. And so actually the organized crime games there have freedom to act, freedom to move, and they can create lots of meth very, very cheaply.

New Zealand pays amongst the world's highest prices for maths. Mm-hmm. So for 2024, uh, price per kilo wholesale Yeah. In the US seven and half thousand dollars kiwi Yeah. In New Zealand, $135,000. And as a minister says, this is a business and where are you gonna go for your profit?

A hundred percent. And so this is where the high market, and this is where the Mexican cartels and in the, it's insane, uh, in the members' question around the DEA report, we're well aware of Mexican cartels identifying New Zealand as being hugely lucrative. Uh, and so we become targeted kind of it the New Zealand end. I think one of the changes, quite frankly, has been the five oh ones that have been deported back here, sophistication that they bring, connectivity that they bring.

We see more diverse trade. We know illicit trade follows legal trade. And so all of that coming together, you start to see this phenomena that that has crack.
